decisions.support_rationale: Two independent cases (px_09_support_1, px_09_support_2) demonstrate convergence of estimates or conclusions from different instruments/methods/sources, directly supporting the claim that such results are robust.

The counterexample case (px_09_counter_3) explicitly shows five reports repeating a single unverified source, creating an apparent consensus that was later found to be fragile, directly matching the claim.

Evidence px_09_counter_3 states the apparent consensus disappeared when the source was corrected, directly supporting the causal claim that such consensus collapses upon correction of the original source.

The experience in px_09_support_1 describes two laboratories using different instruments and samples to estimate a material property, with outcomes converging within a preregistered tolerance, exactly matching the prediction.

The outcome in px_09_support_1 explicitly states estimates converged within a preregistered tolerance, which entails the observable that the difference between estimates is within the tolerance range.
The concept of 'converged within a preregistered tolerance' in px_09_support_1 logically entails the mathematical falsifiability criterion abs(estimate_1 - estimate_2) <= tolerance.

The claim defines the failure condition (non-convergence) for the pattern established in px_09_support_1; while the negative outcome wasn't observed, the logical structure of the falsification statement is supported by the defined success criteria in the evidence.

This observable describes the specific condition (difference exceeds tolerance) that would constitute the failure mode for the pattern observed in px_09_support_1, logically derived from the success criterion.

The case px_09_counter_3 demonstrates that when multiple reports trace to a single unverified source, the pattern of apparent consensus does not hold (it disappeared), directly supporting this falsification statement.
Evidence px_09_counter_3 explicitly states the outcome: 'The apparent consensus disappeared when the source was corrected,' which directly matches the falsification observable.
